data.title: EXG: 9% Yield, International Exposure
data.description:
Looking for international high-yield exposure?

The US $ has taken a beating so far in 2025 - it's down in most major currencies, including the Euro, which is up ~9% vs. the US $.

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (NYSE:EXG) has a portfolio of domestic and foreign common stocks, with an emphasis on dividend-paying stocks. Management writes call options on one or more U.S. and foreign indices to generate cash flow.

Fund Profile:

"The Fund's primary investment objective is to provide current income and gains, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation.

The Fund evaluates returns on an after-tax basis and seeks to minimize and defer federal income taxes incurred by shareholders in connection with their investment in the Fund." (EXG site).

EXG is by far the largest US closed-end fund, "CEF", in the foreign stock category, with a $2.77B asset base, and the largest average daily volume. It began in February 2007 and has 89 holdings.

It uses no leverage, but had 48% of its portfolio overwritten with call options, at an average of 3.70% out of the money, with 5 days left until expiration, as of 3/31/25

Holdings:

With 57% to North America, EXG isn't a purely international investment vehicle, but it does have ~33% exposure to Europe, and ~8% Asia/Pacific exposure.

EXG's sector exposures vs. those of the S&P 500 were overweight in Industrials, Health Care, and Consumer Discretionary; and underweight in Communication Services, Consumer Staples, Materials, and Tech, as of 3/31/25:

Like many equity CEFs, EXG's top 10 holdings included some high-flying Magnificent 7 stocks, such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), as of 3/31/25.

The top 10 positions accounted for 27.6% of the portfolio and also included European-based large stocks such as AstraZeneca, Nestlé, Siemens, and ASML.

Dividends:

EXG pays monthly distributions of $.0657. At $8.49, it yielded 9.29%. It generally goes ex-dividend in mid-month, and pays at the end of the month.

It has a modest 1% 5-year dividend growth average - its most recent hike was in April 2024, when it raised the monthly payout from $.0553 to $.0657.

EXG's fiscal year ends on 10/31. Fiscal year 2024 saw NII declining from $31M to ~$28M, but Net Realized Gains jumped from $8M to ~$118M, and unrealized appreciation more than doubled, from $221M to $452M.

Distributions shifted from ~73% Return of Capital, "ROC" in fiscal 2023, to 35% ROC in fiscal 2024, with Net Assets increasing by ~$364M, vs. $57M in fiscal 2023:

Taxes:

So far, in the fiscal year ending 10/31/2025, distributions are estimated to have come from 14.6% NII, 32% short-term gains, and 53% long-term gains, with no ROC.

Performance:

As of 4/30/25, EXG had outperformed the Morningstar Foreign CEF category on a price and NAV basis. It outperformed in 2019-2021, and 2017, and outperformed on an NAV basis in 2023.

As you'd expect with a covered call strategy, EXG has underperformed the S&P 500 and the MSCI World Index over the past 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods.

Risks:

Option-Selling Risk: As illustrated above, the selling of call options limits upside participation.

Stock Market Risk: The equities held by the fund are subject to market volatility, which could affect the fund's NAV and market price values.

Economic Risk: A recession could dampen demand for some of the fund's holdings, thereby affecting their earnings, and subsequently affecting the market prices and the fund's as well.

Foreign Currency Risk: Just as holding US $-based assets involves currency exposure, holding foreign equities involves risk from foreign currency exchange swings. So far in 2025, the falling dollar has aided certain funds with foreign exposure, but that's not always the case.

Valuations:

Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day.

As of the 5/21/25 close, EXG was selling at a 6.19% discount to its daily NAV/share. That's close to its 5-year average discount of 6.48%, but not nearly as deep as its 1- and 3-year discounts of 9.46% and 8.22%, respectively.

Parting Thoughts:

As of 5/22/25, EXG was 3.75% below its 52-week high, and was overbought in its slow stochastic chart. It has bounced back 8.7% from its April low over the past month-plus.

We rate EXG a HOLD, due to its comparatively average discount to NAV. We prefer to wait for a deeper discount before buying shares.

If you're interested in other high-yield vehicles, we cover them every Friday and Sunday in our articles.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: AT&T And Lumen Technologies Strike A Win-Win Bargain
data.description:
May 21 ended up being a really interesting day for shareholders of both AT&T (NYSE:T) and Lumen Technologies (NYSE:LUMN). This is because, after the market closed, the management teams of both firms announced a major transaction between them. In essence, telecommunications giant AT&T will be purchasing essentially all of the Mass Markets fiber business currently owned by Lumen Technologies in an all-cash transaction valued at $5.75 billion. This move seems to be beneficial to both parties.

In the case of AT&T, it will add substantially to the company's already extensive fiber assets and pave the way for more rapid growth in that space over the next few years. As for Lumen Technologies, this transaction will allow the company to substantially reduce debt and interest payments. Unfortunately, Lumen Technologies still remains in a questionable position from a fundamental perspective. So gauging what potential this business might have moving forward is more complex. But when it comes to AT&T, this is a clear win for those focused on the long haul and further justifies the "strong buy" rating that I have had on the company for quite some time now.

A Big Transaction

According to the press release issued by AT&T, it has agreed to acquire substantially all of Lumen Technologies’ Mass Market fiber business in an all-cash deal valued at $5.75 billion. Even though AT&T is a massive company, this is still a significant deal. The transaction will bring to the company around 1 million fiber subscribers spread across over 4 million fiber locations. These are customers spread across 11 different states, including seven states that the conglomerate currently does not provide fiber services in. While most of these states are fairly low populated Western and Midwestern states, it also includes Oregon and Washington.

One of the first things that might pop up in investors’ minds is what impact this will have on the company and its objectives moving forward. For instance, as you can see in the chart above, management has been working on paying down debt over the last few years. The pro forma figures for the first quarter of 2025 show net of pending transactions that it will receive capital for. For this year, there should not be any impact, since this purchase is not going to close until the first half of next year. But obviously, this will slow the company’s overall net debt reduction. And unless management decides to set aside future capital that it generates from its operations, investors can expect a near term increase in net debt once the transaction closes.

Because this purchase is not going to be completed until sometime in the first half of next year, there's currently no change to AT&T’s plans for capital spending this year. In fact, the company remains committed to repurchasing shares under the $10 billion share buyback program it previously announced, with the end goal of buying back at least $3 billion of stock by the end of this year and a similar amount next year. The company did repurchase $218 million worth of shares in the first quarter of this year. So most of the buying this year still has yet to take place. It's unlikely that this plan will impact the company's dividend either. On an annualized basis, it's currently paying about $8.36 billion back to shareholders in this manner. Nor do I expect it to change the firm's plans to allocate around $22 billion on capital projects this year.

It may seem peculiar that the company is doling out so much cash for these assets when you hear that, according to management, in the 12 to 24 months following the close of the deal, the impact is supposed to be "immaterial" to the company’s EBITDA, adjusted earnings per share, and free cash flow. But this is because management is not focused on short-term benefits. Rather, it's focused on long-term growth. You see, for years now, management has been focused heavily on growing its fiber business. The chart above illustrates the number of fiber broadband connections the company had in the most recent quarter, as well as the number it has had in all of the previous quarters dating back to the first quarter of 2021.

Obviously, this acquisition will add a sizable number of connections to these efforts. But it will also pave the way toward the company achieving an even larger number of fiber passings as time goes on. When I last wrote about the company earlier this month, I detailed how, by the end of this year, the company should meet or exceed its target of hitting 30 million fiber passings nationwide. The objective of the company was to further grow this to 50 million by 2029. Now, however, management sees this number being revised higher to 60 million by the end of 2030. That's almost double what the company has today.

The fact of the matter is that fiber offers significant growth potential to the company from a profit and cash flow perspective. In the first quarter of 2025, AT&T generated $2.1 billion in fiber revenue. That was up 23.5% compared to the $1.7 billion reported the previous year. For all of 2024, fiber revenue amounted to $7.4 billion. And management continues to have comfortably above 261,000 net additions to its network each quarter. The beautiful thing about fiber, it is also bringing higher revenue than the alternative.

During the first quarter of 2025, the firm generated $72.85 per month in ARPU (average revenue per user) from its fiber operations. That represented an increase of 6.2% compared to the $68.61 reported the previous year. This has helped it to grow its EBITDA coming from fiber to $1.3 billion on a quarterly basis. That's up from the $1.1 billion reported at the same time last year. Some of the growth potential comes from the fact that the company sees fiber as a great way to essentially bundle services that it offers to customers. This includes not only for residential customers, but for business customers as well. It can also support the company’s 5G mobile network backhaul. As an example, the company found during the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year that 3.9 million of its fiber subscribers also had mobility subscriptions with the company. That's about 40.2% of all fiber subscribers. That's up from the 3.4 million, or 39.2%, that the company achieved one year earlier. This not only brings in additional revenue. It also creates product stickiness.

Lumen Benefits From This

There is no denying, at least in my opinion, that this will be a long-term benefit to AT&T and its investors. Of course, we will have to wait quite a while to see the full impact this will have on revenue, profits, and cash flows. As for Lumen Technologies, the benefit will be immediate and significant. But before I get into that, I should touch on what Lumen Technologies is and what it does. After all, with a market capitalization as of this writing of $3.91 billion, it's substantially smaller than AT&T. So most investors might not be aware of its existence.

The management team at Lumen Technologies describes the company as a networking firm that's focused on connecting people, data, and applications, securely, quickly, and effortlessly. The firm has four different brands under its belt. The first of these, Lumen, is its flagship brand that includes its Private Connectivity Fabric network architecture, Lumen Digital products, and its priority services such as Edge, Network-as-a-Service, and cybersecurity. This particular part of the company services enterprise and wholesale markets. Then, we have Quantum Fiber, which provides fiber-based broadband services to both residential and small business customers. CenturyLink provides mostly mass marketed copper-based communications services that are, frankly, on the way out long term. And finally, there is Black Lotus Labs, which serves as the company's cyber threat research and intelligence arm.

At the end of its 2024 fiscal year, Lumen Technologies boasted 163,000 fiber on-net buildings and 340,000 route miles of fiber optic cable spread across the markets in which it operates. This includes not only North America, but also part of the Asia Pacific region. Unfortunately, the last few years have not been particularly pleasant for the company. From 2022 through 2024, for instance, revenue dropped from $17.48 billion to $13.11 billion. This was driven by a decline in its Business segment from $13.10 billion to $10.36 billion, and a decline in its Mass Markets segment from $4.38 billion to $2.75 billion.

Some of this drop has been intentional. For instance, in 2023, the company sold off its EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) operations and some of its content delivery network contracts. That caused a roughly $547 million decrease in revenue from 2023 to 2024. And the sale of its Latin America and ILEC businesses the year before that, as well as the sale of its EMEA business, caused revenue to drop by $2.1 billion from 2022 to 2023.

Clearly, this business is no stranger to selling off assets. However, this also has a deleterious effect on profitability and cash flow. As the first chart in this section illustrates, net income has been all over the map. But more consistent has been some of its cash flow data. From 2022 to 2023, operating cash flow plunged from $4.74 billion to $2.16 billion. There was a jump to $4.33 billion last year. We have seen a similar trend when it comes to adjusted operating cash flow, which strips out changes in working capital. But unfortunately, EBITDA has been on a consistent decline, dropping from $6.86 billion to $3.94 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first quarter of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics have all been declining during this window of time. When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management has said that investors should anticipate EBITDA of between $3.2 billion and $3.4 billion. Operating cash flow, meanwhile, should be somewhere between $4.8 billion and $5.2 billion. The good news here is that operating cash flow forecasts are up compared to what the company saw last year. But I don't like seeing the decline in EBITDA.

The reason why management is engaging in this transaction is because the company has a bit of a problem with debt. At the end of the most recent quarter, it had net debt of $15.76 billion. That's an astronomical amount of net debt for a company with a market capitalization of $3.91 billion. This asset sale will allow the company to achieve about $4.8 billion in debt reduction. It should also boost cash flow, both in the near term and in the long run. You see, according to management, it will reduce the company's capital expenditure requirements by $1 billion. The reduction in debt should also lower annual interest expense by roughly $300 million. Based on my own estimates, actual EBITDA for the company should be lower this year by around $333 million if we were to assume that this transaction occurred at the start of this year. But operating cash flow would likely be down by only $33 million.

This is a small price to pay for a substantial reduction in leverage. You see, without the deal taking place, management estimated that the net leverage ratio for the business would be 4.9 by the end of this year. That compares to the 4.0 we get using data from the end of 2024. But adjusted for this sale, the net leverage ratio for the business should be closer to 3.9. On top of reducing overall debt by $4.8 billion, this transaction will reduce the 2029 and 2030 maturities that the company has coming due by 40%. This, and the improvement in cash flows, should in theory give the business some additional breathing room.

It also, in management's eyes, will help the company achieve its goal of focusing more on its enterprise operations. By retaining its core fiber infrastructure that focuses on enterprise customers, the company still has an opportunity to achieve growth in the long run. As evidenced by the large portion of revenue that comes from its Business segment compared to the rest of the firm, it's clear that this is just another move to further its ambitions in this regard. In fact, management hopes to achieve 47 million intercity fiber miles by the year 2028. This is up from 16.6 million that the company said that it would have by the end of this year.

There seems to be tremendous opportunity here for growth, particularly with the rise of AI and the demand it could have on infrastructure. On top of growth and this reduction in leverage that this transaction will bring to the table, it's also worth pointing out that management has been making some other changes as well. For instance, when it announced financial results for the first quarter of this year in early May, the company stated that it's making major changes to parts of its business, including unifying its network architecture and leveraging AI for automation purposes. The end goal here is to achieve annual run rate cost savings of $250 million by the end of 2025. And by 2027, it expects savings to grow to $1 billion. The company also continues to focus on other opportunities, such as its collaboration with Google Cloud to offer direct fiber access with speeds of up to 400 gigabytes per second. Management's claim is that this opens up a new $15 billion total addressable market opportunity for shareholders. All told, management believes that the enterprise market in the space in which it operates is worth around $80 billion.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned that the impact on the company's bottom line from this purchase alone should be a slight reduction in both operating cash flow and EBITDA. Admittedly, this is just based on taking the estimated revenue from the sale of these assets and stripping out a proportional amount of EBITDA. Because management has not provided any concrete numbers themselves, the actual end result could be different. Having said that, the one downside that I see from this is that it will reduce one of the few areas of growth that the company has seen in recent years.

From 2022 to 2024, fiber broadband revenue under the Mass Markets segment for the company grew from $604 million to $736 million. This was in spite of some declines caused by other changes that the company made, such as a $41 million decrease from 2022 to 2023 caused by the firm's sale of its ILEC business. So actual organic revenue would have grown even faster, with management attributing this to an increase in the number of fiber customers and the continued increase in enabled locations for the firm’s Quantum Fiber buildout. For the first quarter of this year, fiber broadband revenue hit $209 million. That's up from the $170 million you reported the previous year.

Now, it's worth mentioning that not all of this revenue will disappear because of this transaction. Keep in mind that management said that the deal includes "substantially all" of the assets included in these operations. However, according to management, over the trailing four quarters ending in the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, total revenue that's being sold off will amount to $750 million. But according to my calculation, the fiber broadband operations of the company as a whole brought in $775 million during this window of time. So even though it won't be material, the company will be retaining some of the revenue from these operations. Regardless, it's bittersweet to see such a rapidly growing piece of the pie be sold off. But if it means that the company can reduce debt, reduce capital spending to boost free cash flows, and focus on its larger market, then the deal is probably worth it.

As for the value of the company, in the chart above, you can see how shares of the business are priced on both a price to operating cash flow basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis using estimates for 2025 and historical results for 2024. This chart also shows my estimate of the value for the company based on the pro forma figures that strip out this additional net debt and the estimated changes in cash flows as a result of the deal. Notably, on an EV to EBITDA basis, Lumen Technologies is getting cheaper because of this transaction.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I'm a fan of this transaction. In the long run, this should bode well for AT&T and its shareholders. It will further the company's growth initiatives, specifically in areas where it's most focused and has the greatest potential. The only downside is that it will increase leverage compared to not making the purchase and won't have a material impact on revenue, profits, or cash flows, in the near term. But as a long-term investor, I don't mind that. As for Lumen Technologies, the transaction is more complicated. The good news is that the net benefit for the company should be positive because of the debt reduction and the reduction in capital expenditure requirements moving forward. At the end of the day, I believe that maintaining a "strong buy" rating on AT&T is appropriate. And as for Lumen Technologies, I believe that a "buy" rating makes sense.

data.title: FBTC: Breaking Out On Fiat Fears
data.description:
The Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund ETF (BATS:FBTC) broke to new all-time highs this week, exceeding the $94.8 peak set in December of last year. This article explores the drivers behind the break-out, and why it may not be a good idea to chase the rally at these levels.

A Quick Look at FBTC

FBTC was launched in January 2024 and has become one of the most popular Bitcoin ETFs. Its low expense ratio of 0.25% and excellent liquidity are an attraction, as is the well-known and trusted name behind the product, "Fidelity." To be completely honest, there is very little to recommend it above its main competitor, the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), which has a slightly better performance (only 0.13% since inception and probably from a tracking error), and higher AUM.

However, if you have a large exposure to Bitcoin, I would consider FBTC as an alternative to IBIT and spread the risk between the two funds. One consideration is that they have different custodians and Fidelity is self-custodian.

Driving the Break-out

FBTC often has a high-beta correlation to other risk assets such as stocks and the weakness in the S&P500 (SPY) in Q1 was replicated in FBTC. Both bottomed on the exact same day, April 7th.

There are periods where FBTC will out or under-perform due to various reasons. This was evident following the election when the new administration's crypto-friendly stance sparked a large rally. It was also the case this week as FBTC and SPY have moved in opposite directions and FBTC broke to a new all-time high.

The driver this week appears to be Moody's downgrade of US government debt. This sparked a rise in yields as the risk of buying/holding US Treasuries has increased. It also dropped the US dollar, and it was notable that when stocks and bonds recovered on Monday 19th May (the first trading session after the downgrade was announced), the US dollar stayed weak. After a feeble recovery in May, it looks to be heading for further lows.

Here is some of the rationale for the downgrade:

Without adjustments to taxation and spending, we expect budget flexibility to remain limited, with mandatory spending, including interest expense, projected to rise to around 78% of total spending by 2035 from about 73% in 2024. If the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act is extended, which is our base case, it will add around $4 trillion to the federal fiscal primary (excluding interest payments) deficit over the next decade.

As a result, we expect federal deficits to widen, reaching nearly 9% of GDP by 2035, up from 6.4% in 2024, driven mainly by increased interest payments on debt, rising entitlement spending, and relatively low revenue generation. We anticipate that the federal debt burden will rise to about 134% of GDP by 2035, compared to 98% in 2024.

And here's a shocking projection:

Federal interest payments are likely to absorb around 30% of revenue by 2035, up from about 18% in 2024 and 9% in 2021.

None of this is particularly new - Fitch, and Standard and Poor's already downgraded the credit rating years ago, and it was a matter of time before Moody's followed suit. But this puts some worrying calculations back in the spotlight. In the long term, the debt pile will erode the value of the dollar and all other fiat currencies. Crypto (and FBTC) is attractive as it has no sovereign risk, no debt, and no dilution. This is why it diverged from other risk assets this week and broke to a new all-time high.

A Temporary Tailwind

Breaking to new highs when stocks are moving in the other direction is impressive, but we don't want to see the two asset classes diverge for too long as they tend to rally and correct together over the longer term.

A sustained period of FBTC outperformance would likely need a significant and new driver. A strategic Bitcoin reserve would fit the bill, but that now seems a dead-end given the project has to be budget-neutral.

As already outlined, the reasons for the divergence this week are not new developments - crypto's main attraction has always been its independence from what many see as a doomed system of fiat currencies. Moody's downgrade may have brought some issues to the fore again, but nothing specific sparked the move.

In short, I do not expect the divergence to last very long, and since stocks look to be rolling over - or at least unlikely to break to new highs any time soon - the break-out in FBTC may also stall. As I am writing this, I see both stocks and bitcoin have dropped as President Trump has threatened the EU with 50% tariffs. This isn't necessarily bearish, but we may see a consolidation like we did the last time Bitcoin approached a previous all-time high. The current pattern and the larger-scale pattern from 2022-2025 are quite similar.

Implications

The main takeaway is to not get carried away with this break-out. The outlook for FBTC is bullish, but I would not rush in to buy at new all-time highs. A pullback to the gap around $85 would be a more compelling buying opportunity over the summer.

For those holding long-term, this week's developments underpin why crypto is a good investment, but don't really change anything.

Conclusions

FBTC and stocks diverged this week following the Moody's downgrade. While the rally was welcome for bitcoin bulls, the drivers do not look like igniting a sustained divergence as the downgrade was expected and not due to anything particularly new.

Without a "risk on" environment that also lifts stocks, the break-out may not be sustained and a drift-down/consolidation looks likely. FBTC is long-term bullish, and I rate it a "hold," but I would wait to initiate new positions nearer $85.

data.title: Did You Think It Was Over? New Tariffs Are Coming (And Maybe Another Crash)
data.description:
Apple and Europe Under Scrutiny

After a disastrous beginning in April, the suspension of tariffs and the de-escalation with China led me to believe (perhaps mistakenly) that the situation was stabilizing. Let's be clear, tariffs have not been eliminated even after the suspension; currently, there is a universal tariff of 10% on all imports and 30% on imports from China. However, compared to 145%, significant improvements had been made which spread enthusiasm across the markets.

The tariff issue was moving towards a resolution, and the only concern remaining was whether the tariffs currently in place were still too high to generate high inflation. We will only know for sure in a few months' time, but today's events have left me rather perplexed.

President Trump has announced that if Apple Inc. (AAPL) does not manufacture its iPhones sold in the US (> 60 million per year) domestically, it will face a tariff of at least 25%. This statement comes after several weeks of meetings between President Trump and Apple CEO Tim Cook, during which no agreement was reached between the two parties. Due to US-China tensions, Apple wants to gradually move production of its iPhones sold in the US to India (where it already has multiple factories), but this does not seem to particularly please President Trump. One of his main goals is to revive American manufacturing, and what better way to do so than to have one of the best American companies in history manufacture its products domestically? The point is that Apple clearly does not manufacture its iPhones in the US because of the high cost of labor compared to China and India. It is not a question of wanting to, but of being unable to.

At the time of writing, the stock is down about 3%, but as far as I am concerned, this is perhaps too little given the seriousness of the situation. Apple does not seem willing to move its production to the US, as there is currently no supply chain to produce an iPhone and huge investments would be required. We cannot know exactly how much an iPhone produced in America would cost; certainly much more than the current price, and demand would take a hit.

It is a very complicated situation because this ‘conflict’ is uneven. Unlike China, which has the leverage to stand up to the US, Apple is a company and cannot win against the country where it is based. If no agreement is reached between Apple and the President, the situation could escalate, given that he referred to a tariff of at least 25%. Therefore, if the situation escalates, it could reach higher levels and sink Apple. I don't know how far it will go, but what is certain is that this decision highlights a rather worrying aspect that does not only concern Apple.

If one of the best companies in the country can be targeted and sunk because it does not manufacture its products internally, no company is truly safe.

President Trump recently announced his disappointment with Walmart after the company said it would raise prices due to tariffs. Should shareholders be worried at this point?

Mattel fared even worse when CEO Ynon Kreiz announced that they were considering producing fewer toys in China and moving to other countries, but not the US.

That’s OK, let him go, and we’ll put a 100% tariff on his toys, and he won’t sell one toy in the United States, and that’s their biggest market.

President Trump about production shift not in the U.S.

In an economic environment where companies can be targeted and forced to do what they don't want to do, there is a risk of capital flight. Investors want clarity, and a lack of trust discourages them from buying companies in that country. A similar situation occurred a few years ago in China, where the government intervened directly in the expansion plans of the country's top companies. Ant Group's IPO was canceled, Alibaba and Tencent were fined and forced to review their internal corporate policies. In addition, a series of rules were issued for the common good, but these did not represent the good of those companies. For Apple, manufacturing in the US is not the best economic choice, but it may be forced to do so.

An ad hoc sanction against Apple seems to be the beginning of a context in which companies can operate freely, but only up to a certain limit. This limit is represented by President Trump's will, but this type of situation does not please the financial markets. In fact, futures on the major stock indices are in sharp decline.

In addition to the Apple issue, a few minutes later, there was also an announcement for Europe.

If an agreement is not reached quickly, Europe risks facing tariffs of 50% starting June 1; these would even be higher than those imposed on China. Given the political fragmentation of European countries, I find it difficult to see how an agreement can be reached in such a short time.

This situation, combined with all the others, is creating serious doubts among investors, and the market does not like uncertainty.

In recent weeks, we have been approaching all-time highs, but this news leads me to believe that the tariff issue will continue for some time. It is not just a matter of ‘Liberation Day’, it is something much bigger and more complex. Individual companies that do not follow the President's will and countries that are slow to negotiate are now under pressure. Who will be the next target?

data.title: Inflation Anxiety And The 'Big Beautiful Bill'
data.description:
The mega spending bill that the House passed now awaits debate in the Senate. A key issue for markets is how, or if, the legislation will be amended with respect to projections that, if passed, the bill will deepen an already hefty federal deficit in the years ahead and thereby stoke inflation concerns as the government’s borrowing needs increase.

“I’m hoping now we’ll actually start looking at reality,” said Sen. Ron Johnson (R-Wis.). “I know everybody wants to go to Disney World, but we just can’t afford it.”

BBC reports: “At present, the US deficit – the difference between what the government spends and how much it collects in taxes – is $1.9 trillion. The tax cuts proposed in Trump’s bill could add $3.8 trillion to the deficit over the next decade, according to the Congressional Budget Office.”

Meanwhile, three key market indicators deserve close attention for real-time feedback for inflation expectations:

  • The Treasury market’s inflation-indexed 5-year yield
  • The 5-Year breakeven inflation rate
  • The 5-Year, 5-Year Forward Inflation Expectation Rate

All three inflation metrics have been trending up lately but remain middling relative to recent history. An upside breakout by one or more of these yields would be a sign that an already anxious market is becoming more concerned about inflation.

“Longer term, yeah, [the spending bill] blows out the deficit. Long term, for the market, it’s bad news. Yields are going higher, which means prices are going down because Treasuries are becoming incrementally less appealing and trustworthy, as our budget deficit stays extremely high for a very long period of time with no signs of it going back to normal,” said Jed Ellerbroek, portfolio manager at Argent Capital Management.

Although investors can debate the merits of Ellerbroek’s analysis, market data is another story. If the inflation indicators continue rising and take out previous highs, the writing will be on the wall. Meantime, the ball is in the Senate’s court.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC: Widely Underrated
data.description:
In my last piece on business development company Goldman Sachs BDC Inc. (NYSE:GSBD), I recommended the BDC’s stock as a ‘Buy’ to passive income investors. This stock classification was supported by Goldman Sachs BDC lowering its incentive fee, which created wiggle room for the business development company’s net investment income and dividend payout ratio.

In Q1'25, Goldman Sachs BDC did not fully cover its total dividend with net investment income, but because of the change in the dividend composition (which I discussed in my last piece as well), I am not concerned about issues of sustainability.

The stock of Goldman Sachs BDC is still available for passive income investors at a discount to net asset value, and there is a potential re-rating opportunity in the case that Goldman Sachs BDC can improve its portfolio performance and lower its non-accrual ratio in 2025.

Portfolio Composition And Non-Accrual Ratio

Goldman Sachs BDC is a 100% floating-rate business development company with 91% of its investments allocated to Senior Secured First Lien Deb, the highest form of debt in a company’s capital stack. As of March 31, 2025, the business development company owned total assets valued at $3.38 billion, reflecting a QoQ decrease of 2.6%.

Goldman Sachs BDC has suffered deterioration of its credit quality due to an increase in loans that didn’t pay interest as laid out in the underlying loan agreement, which in turn has pressured the company’s net investment income and caused an increase in the BDC’s non-accrual ratio. In Q1'25, Goldman Sachs’s non-accrual ratio stood at 1.9% based on fair value, compared to 2.0% in the prior quarter.

Goldman Sachs BDC has seen a steady decrease in its gross originations in the last three quarters and negative net funded activity in three out of the last five quarters. As a consequence, Goldman Sachs BDC suffered a consistent decline in its portfolio income metrics as well.

Goldman Sachs BDC is experiencing a broad decline in income, ranging from total net investment income to net investment income, thanks to the company losing portfolio value to lower new originations and high repayments as well as non-accrual problems.

In Q1'25, Goldman Sachs BDC produced $49.6 million in net investment income ($48.8 million in adjusted net investment income) which reflected an 18% YoY decrease. The BDC’s total investment income fell a more moderate 13% YoY, but the trend here is clearly also negative.

Why The Dividend Pay-Out Ratio Is Poised To Drop

In the first quarter, Goldman Sachs BDC earned $0.42 per share in net investment income and therefore did not fully cover its total dividend pay-out of $0.48 per share; the implied dividend pay-out ratio was 114%.

That being said, passive income investors need to understand that Goldman Sachs BDC changed its dividend composition in the previous quarter and has now set a base dividend of $0.32 per share, which in the first quarter was supplemented by a special dividend of $0.16 per share. After the end of the first quarter, the business development company also declared a $0.05 per share special dividend.

Here is what Goldman Sachs BDC’s dividend payout stats look like for the last five quarters:

In terms of LTM dividend pay-out, Goldman Sachs BDC is still doing relatively well and the business development company has considerable potential to improve its dividend pay-out now that it modified its dividend structure: The BDC is going to pay a $0.32 per share per quarter dividend moving forward. This dividend will be supplemented by variable payments in the amount of at least 50% of NII exceeding its base dividend.

Compared to a small core group of business development companies, Goldman Sachs BDC does not look particularly bad, with an LTM dividend pay-out ratio of 88% vs. a group average of 90%.

A Re-Rating Candidate If Credit Quality Improves

The reason why I still recommend Goldman Sachs BDC despite its high non-accrual ratio is that the business development company is still selling at a big discount to net asset value, which creates a valuation upside in the event that Goldman Sachs BDC can rectify its credit quality.

Goldman Sachs BDC is one of the few business development companies that are still selling at a discount to the last reported net asset value. As of March 31, 2025, Goldman Sachs BDC’s net asset value amounted to $13.20 per share, reflecting a decrease of 1.6% QoQ.

Goldman Sachs BDC is the lowest-priced business development company in the BDC peer group with a Price-To-NAV ratio of 0.84x vs. a peer group average Price-To-NAV ratio of 1.02x.

The peer group average Price-To-NAV ratio, however, tilted upward primarily because of the inclusion of Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) which is selling for a lofty 17% premium to net asset value.

Because Goldman Sachs BDC has the highest discount to net asset value, the business development company also has the highest net investment income yield, as measured by the company’s present stock price. With an NII yield of 14.9%, Goldman Sachs BDC has a 26% higher yield than the peer average, making it potentially a more promising investment for passive income investors than higher-priced business development companies.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be Bearish

Goldman Sachs BDC already changed its dividend composition in the prior quarter, as I have explained in the section about the dividend payout ratio, so the BDC’s dividend coverage itself is not a major reason for concern for me.

What is of concern to me, is that Goldman Sachs BDC still has a fairly high non-accrual ratio of 1.9% and is seeing a negative income trend across the board.

My Takeaway

Taking both positives and negatives from Goldman Sachs BDC’s Q1'25 earnings presentation into consideration, I come to the conclusion that Goldman Sachs BDC is still worth owning as a passive income vehicle.

Goldman Sachs BDC is suffering a broad decline in income metrics, which is the result of higher repayments, lower new originations, and performance headwinds for existing loans.

Though the business development company did not earn its dividend with net investment income in Q1'25 and the non-accrual ratio is higher than average, I think that the discount to NAV as well as the high NII yield compensate passive income investors here for the higher investment risk.

data.title: Lazard International Quality Growth Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

  • Driven by Europe, and helped by stronger currencies, international equities rose strongly.
  • International equities outperformed equities in the United States by the largest quarterly amount in more than two decades.
  • European defense spending and potential German fiscal spending, both significant, contributed to strong European returns.
  • Concerns over tariffs contributed to higher inflation expectations. Amidst higher rates, Value significantly outperformed as banks extended their run within financials, followed by other outperforming sectors like energy and utilities. Quality significantly lagged the market during the last two quarters.

Led by European corporate fundamentals, increasing optimism around fiscal stimulus, and strengthening currencies, international equities staged the largest quarterly outperformance over US equities in over two decades.

Valuations have been inexpensive for international equities for several years. International equities experienced two large changes this year that could change that discount. The first was DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), a Chinese artificial intelligence (AI) company, announcing in January that it could offer open-source, high-performance AI models at a fraction of the cost of traditional AI giants. This caused competitors to pause and re-evaluate how much they spend on training AI models, and investors to rethink the valuations they are willing to pay for AI-related companies. While the concentration of the US market remains significantly tilted to many of these AI- related companies, whose stretched valuations are dependent on continued significant investment spend, DeepSeek has called some of that into question. As a result, global investors began to shift money from the expensive US market to the less expensive international markets.

Soon after the DeepSeek competitive revaluation, economic forecasts began to predict that global GDP could be negatively affected, and US tariffs could further impact the longer-term inflation outlook.

The tectonic shift in geopolitics continued as the US appeared to step back from its role of providing defense support for European allies. At the same time, Friedrich Merz, winner of the German election, pledged to focus on European cooperation. His spending and investment plan for Germany stands in stark contrast to the historic fiscal conservatism of German governments. The relatively low level of German public debt has enabled Merz to pivot and potentially provide the much-needed fiscal stimulus to Europe’s largest economy. The potential for a multi-year increase in fiscal stimulus in Germany, targeted at transport, communication, digital and power infrastructure, coupled with a significantly higher level of targeted defense spending across Europe, gave some investors hope that this is the fiscal unification moment that Europe has needed.

Geopolitical volatility and the shift in relative growth rates between the US and Europe have also moved bond markets and raised expectations for higher future rates.

Relatively higher bond yields (the discount rate for equities), coupled with lower expectations for growth in traditional Growth sectors such as technology and consumer discretionary, have driven a significant move toward Value, where the investment horizon is usually shorter, and away from Quality, where the investment horizon is usually longer. Within financials, the banking industry continued its extraordinary performance, leading the All Country World ex-US Index by 6% during the quarter and 17% over the past year. Other traditional Value sectors, such as energy and utilities, also outperformed during the quarter.

In this shifting environment of higher rates and slower growth expectations, Quality companies lagged Value by a wide margin. While the fundamental earnings power of certain quality companies we own continues to rise more than the market, the derating of the valuation has been significant. Over the past six months, international quality investors have seen two of the worst four quarters for Quality since 2001.

Portfolio Review

During the first quarter, the Lazard International Quality Growth Portfolio rose 1.9% (net of fees1), lagging the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index and the MSCI All Country World ex-US Growth Index, which rose 5.2% and 2.0%, respectively. Our investment philosophy is to seek the highest quality companies. We define these companies as those we believe can generate the highest levels of financial productivity with enduring competitive advantages that will enable them to continue delivering that high financial productivity well into the future and those that can reinvest for growth.

We expect the Portfolio to perform well in most market environments, driven by the consistency with which Quality has tended to perform as a style over many years. When economies are growing, we would expect performance to be driven by the compounding of cash flows as the companies we hold reinvest to drive growth. In recessionary environments, we would expect the high-quality characteristics, coupled with the barriers to competition the companies possess, to allow them to maintain pricing power and see smaller declines in earnings.

When compared to the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index, we expect to participate, but potentially lag in short-term, low-quality led market environments. When compared to the MSCI All Country World ex-US Growth Index, we expect to participate, but potentially lag a market led significantly by unprofitable momentum growth companies, which are most often associated with excessively low interest rates environments.

The past two quarters were two of the strongest quarters favoring the Value style that we have seen in decades.

During the first quarter, our Portfolio companies experienced positive EPS revisions that were stronger than the market. What hurt performance was the derating of the valuation of the companies compared to the market. When looking at each stock in the Portfolio, roughly two-thirds saw their forward EPS estimates rise more than the market. However, nearly the same number saw valuations fall more than the market.

Net-of-fees performance reflects the deduction of the highest tier investment management fee charged based on Lazard’s stand institutional separate account fee schedule for the Lazard International Quality Growth strategy, which is 0.75% per annum.

Positives

Stock selection in the information technology sector contributed positively to relative returns.

  • Based in Israel, Check Point Software Technologies (CHKP) (2.8% weighting in the Portfolio) is a leading global, supplier of cybersecurity products and services to both governments and corporations. Shares rose after the company reported fourth quarter and full-year results. Gross margin came in at 89% for both the quarter and the full-year, while net margin came in at 43% and 41%, respectively, for the quarter and the year, highlighting industry-leading profitability. Total billings (revenue) grew 11% year-over- year, and RPO (remaining performance obligation), which reflects non-cancelable products or services that are yet to be reflected as revenue, grew 12% year-over-year. These results, which highlighted industry-leading profitability, are supportive of our investment thesis.
  • TOTVS (OTCPK:TTVSY) (1.6% weighting in the Portfolio) is the dominant provider of ERP (enterprise resource planning) solutions in Brazil and is expanding into CRM (customer relationship management) solutions. Brazil has a complicated tax regulation, which creates a high entry barrier for foreign ERP software companies and helps TOTVS maintain its dominant place in the market. TOTVS’ has approximately a 97% retention rate, and annualized recurring revenue accounts for more than 90% of its total revenue, creating a solid base to grow from. Shares rose after the company reported consensus-beating fourth-quarter results, where strong group performance was supported by broad-based segment performance and recurring revenue growth supported margin expansion.

Stock specific contributors to relative returns included:

  • Scout24 (OTCPK:SCOTF) (2.0% weighting in the Portfolio), a German holding company for internet businesses with subsidiaries in finance, accounting, controlling, internal audit, risk management, compliance, strategy, etc. The ImmoScout24 is a digital marketplace for residential and commercial real estate. Shares rose after the company’s fourth quarter results highlighted strong performance across all businesses. Management guided for 12-14% revenue growth in 2025, ahead of consensus estimates, and commented on improving macro conditions. They expect an improvement in transactions in the property market to translate to greater demand for the company’s core agent subscription products (e.g., recurring charges for premium packages with better listing placement) and pay per add (PPA) spend (charged for each listing). We trimmed the portfolio’s position as a source of funds for InterContinental Hotels Group.

Negatives

Close to half the underperformance during the quarter came from allocation, providing more evidence that the Value style was dominant this quarter. In fact, the combination of our underweight to banks and our overweights to commercial services (an industry comprised of many high quality, asset-light businesses) and technology cost roughly 70% of the total underperformance in the quarter relative to the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index.

There were some stock specific detractors from performance.

  • Companies with higher US exposure:

Domiciled in Ireland, Accenture (ACN) (2.7% weighting in the Portfolio) is one of the highest-quality IT services companies benefiting from the global transformation of digital infrastructure. While recent results came in slightly ahead of consensus expectations, shares underperformed on macroeconomic uncertainty and softer discretionary spending conditions. Despite this, Accenture’s trailing 12-month AI bookings remain 15-20% above revenue run rates, and the backlog continues to grow. In the long term, we expect Accenture to continue to lead customers through the digital disruption taking place in all industries, and this differentiation coupled with the company’s unique vertical capabilities should enable strong growth and market share gains.

Based in Japan, Recruit (OTCPK:RCRRF) (1.8% weighting in the Portfolio) owns the leading online job recruiting platforms, including Indeed, Glassdoor, and RFG Staffing. In Japan, it is the leader in online listing/matching solutions for staffing, real estate, beauty, and bridal, as well as a host of services. The company has maintained pricing power despite a reduction in job openings, demonstrating strong barriers to competition, which we think will allow returns to be sustained. Recruit implemented predictive supply-demand models to optimize job posting prices based on location and job type, and is also leveraging AI to automate job searches and improve internal productivity. It has a large job posting and candidate data, which it feeds into its algorithm to refine its search models. Recruit has placed focus on increasing the take rates (commission) by shifting more customers from freemium to paying models. To do so, Recruit continues to demonstrate a clear return on investment (achieved through case studies, testimonials, and data-driven insights) to its customers. Despite reporting third quarter results that came in slightly ahead of consensus expectations, shares underperformed because of investors’ concerns over a lower number of job openings. We think the share price reaction is overdone and not reflective of the fundamentals of the company. Recruit generates nearly a 20%, and improving, ROE with a net cash balance sheet and, we think, it is well-positioned for ample growth.

  • Spirits company:

Based in the United Kingdom, Diageo (DEO) (1.8% weighting in the Portfolio) is the leader in the international spirits’ industry. It has leading positions in the most attractive spirits categories (tequila and whiskey) and exposure to high-growth markets like India (where it has the number one market share). We think Diageo is poised to benefit from continued strong growth of the spirits’ industry, and it has improved its portfolio of brands and its premium weight, allowing it to potentially outperform the industry growth rate consistently. The moat of the business is wide, with two-thirds of sales in aged liquid that requires significant strategic inventories and infrastructure investments. Despite reporting first-half results that saw organic revenue growth beat consensus expectations, the timing of Diageo’s reporting coincided with tariff news. As a result, management felt it was not appropriate to provide a new medium-term guidance target at the time of the release, causing the shares to underperform. We think the impact of tariffs is already priced into the shares, and continue to see favorable upside for Diageo. The industry has an attractive growth outlook (mid-single-digit growth) driven by product mix in developed markets and increasing per capita consumption in emerging markets. Margins and returns are high, with a wide moat, especially for companies that primarily sell aged spirits (such as Diageo). Diageo continues to gain market share and improve free cash flow, and we think it can continue to do so going forward.

Recent Activity and Trade Rationale

Buys

  • InterContinental Hotels Group (IHG) (1.7% weighting in the Portfolio), is the fourth-largest hotel group globally by rooms and system size and one of the few global asset-light hotel models based in the United Kingdom.
  • Japan-based Recruit (1.8% weighting in the Portfolio), which owns the leading online job recruiting platforms, including Indeed, Glassdoor, and RFG Staffing.

Sells

  • Based in Canada, BRP (DOOO) (0.0% weighting in the Portfolio) is the leading power sports OEM (original equipment manufacturer) globally. We exited our position as weaker than expected barriers to competition broke our investment thesis.
  • Based in Japan, Shimano (OTCPK:SHMDF) (0.0% weighting in the Portfolio) is a leading high-end bicycle components manufacturer. We exited our position as a source of funds for Recruit.

Outlook

  • Heightened geopolitical uncertainty is likely to persist, and may result in slower investment and consumption.
  • As slower growth and higher inflation fears increase, we expect quality companies to be well-positioned to resume leadership.
  • The historical magnitude of the underperformance of quality suggests the style may outperform going forward.
  • The drivers of US outperformance are diminishing or reversing.
  • European fiscal stimulus plans could provide continued tailwind for European equities.
  • International valuations remain attractive compared to those of the US.

Tariffs and defense spending were major points of discussion in the first quarter. Company managements have attempted to determine the impact of tariffs on their businesses. From sales demand to pricing to margins, tariffs and defense spending affect many facets of investment planning and decision-making. In the near term, the likely outcome is that management teams will remain cautious and wait to see how the situation sorts out. In the meantime, sales and profits could be negatively impacted. We have been speaking with companies in our Portfolio, trying to assess how these new policies will impact their businesses.

One likely outcome of these new policies is slower growth and rising inflation. In this environment, investors initially reached for precious metals as a real asset store of value, and we have seen gold and silver rally substantially in the first quarter. Banks also rallied significantly, as they are also viewed as beneficiaries as interest rates rise, increasing their net interest margins, and investors believe loan demand will remain robust as businesses seek to capitalize on the inflationary environment.

What happens from here? We believe that companies with stronger pricing power will more likely be able to pass along higher costs to their customers without seeing demand deteriorate and will likely have a higher probability of maintaining their margins.

Companies with the widest moats, the highest financial productivity, and the opportunity to reinvest for growth are likely to resume leadership. During the past six months, Quality businesses derated and underperformed by more than 5%. In the past quarter century, every time quality underperformed by that magnitude or more, it outperformed in the subsequent period. We believe companies that can organically grow earnings while maintaining margins and profitability will be leaders going forward, and, therefore, we expect history to repeat.

data.title: Merck: Defensive Yield Meets Long-Term Optionality
data.description:
Merck & Co. (NYSE:MRK) is a defensive play with good dividend prospects and long-term optionality, making it a patient GARP style buy. Investors should not expect immediate excitement barring term technical bounce backs. I position Merck as a stable compounder with low speculative biotech risks - a core portfolio stabilizer in a larger portfolio and certainly in your biotech portfolio.

The Keytruda Question

Concentration risk is high on Keytruda. Close to 45% of all revenue came from the cancer drug in Q1 2025. The Keytruda Loss of Exclusivity (LOE) is slated to start in 2028, leading to biosimilars eroding the market share. Although biologics like Keytruda will likely see slower erosion than small molecule drugs, a significant revenue gap is evident.

In 2024, total Keytruda sales were $29.5b. I have modeled the impact aggressively to study whether valuations and prices reflect the cliff effect. I have assumed that the LOE will cause a sharp decline in revenue over the first three years after patent expiration, i.e., revenue drops to approximately 50% in year 1, 25% in year 2, and 15% in year 3. Additional revenue erosion is considered as a further 20% reduction in the remaining revenue each year, reflecting market and pricing pressures beyond the initial LOE impact.

What is Merck doing about it? Apart from the pipeline efforts to replace the hole, in terms of Keytruda itself, efforts are ongoing to extend the use to early-stage cancers and use cases less vulnerable to replacement by biosimilars.

Merck is also actively advancing its pipeline across several therapeutic areas. In oncology, the company is developing promising candidates such as Winrevair, the V940 personalized mRNA cancer vaccine in partnership with Moderna, and T-cell engagers through its acquisition of Harpoon Therapeutics. In immunology, Merck has strengthened its position with acquisitions like Prometheus Biosciences, which focuses on inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) and lupus.

The aggressive revenue erosion of ~40% does not factor in the pipeline replacement potential at all, as the P/S ratio (and share prices) have both corrected by a similar ~40% in the past year. I acknowledge that the loss of market-leading products is more damage to valuations than my linear expectation. Also, the market is skeptical about pipeline replacements. In some cases, so am I. Recently, I have had my concerns about the operational challenges to get to Moderna cancer treatment's commercialization. However, that concern is from a 2-3 year window view. You look at Keytruda impacts starting 2028 and three years out from there, and long-term prospects improve as there is enough time to work out the hurdles.

But more in defense of my viewpoint, no pipeline replacement and aggressive revenue losses (unlikely) should at worst balance out that premium valuation erosion. In short, valuations are supportive. The extent of support is extremely good in my view. If there are counterarguments to that, even then, the bottom is not far from current levels.

The Gardasil Conundrum

It would have been great if all the fall in MRK could be attributed to the Keytruda issue. Obviously, that is not the case. Markets are bearish because of other impending short-term concerns as well. The second leading offering from Merck, Gardasil, has been facing demand headwinds in China. In Q1 2025, sales were reported at $193m vs $1.3b in Q1 2024. Further shipments to China have been temporarily halted to stabilize the supply.

I believe the China sales are low enough to assume the bottom. Despite the lows, the drop in overall Gardasil sales was a more manageable 40% YoY, supported by positive 14% growth ex-China (demand in Japan, US, and pricing improvements). If the growth ex-China is retained and even if China does not recover, we could be looking at overall Gardasil growth from here on. I am even discounting the bounce-back potential in China from the approval for use in males.

Revenue and Margin Impacts

Revenue did see a marginally negative YoY change in Q1 2025, but there is not much room for further significant revenue downsides in China. There is, admittedly, no major short-term catalyst despite a promising long-term pipeline. Nothing exciting may be expected in the upcoming quarters, but the buy thesis hinges on the long-term optionality with limited downside from here, not short-term triggers.

In fact, gross margins in Q1 2025 had a good story to tell. A favorable product mix (with falling Gardasil sales, Keytruda improved the product mix in favor of higher margins) and a decrease noted in operating expenses, meant margin improvements without a noticeable dent in the top line.

Management guidance is encouraging from the fact that revenue targets have not been cut. Gross margin rates for the full year have been revised downwards from 82.5% to 82% considering tariff impacts of $200m. In fact, even if the tariff uncertainty ends up adverse to Merck's assumptions, which is far lower an impact than I had anticipated, the margins are not expected to be impacted materially. Merck is also looking at low China exposure anyway at this point. There cannot be major shocks due to adverse tariffs for Merck at this point.

Long-Term Growth Drivers

Merck does have a lot of immediate headwinds, including pricing pressure from the US government for regulated pharma prices. But I think a large part of the underperformance for MRK was due to cyclical rotation into AI-led tech rallies, rotating money out of defensives. An upcoming macro uncertainty regime could pull money back into undervalued defensives with good income opportunities.

More importantly, the long-term optionality for Merck is compelling. To diversify beyond oncology, the company has completed targeted acquisitions, enabling a foray into new therapeutic areas such as cardiometabolic and immunology. Its oncology pipeline remains robust, with assets including TIGIT and LAG-3 inhibitors, as well as antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs). In vaccines, Merck is advancing candidates for RSV and CMV, although near-term growth has been weighed down by a sharp drop in Gardasil sales in China. The company is also leveraging AI to accelerate drug development and improve success rates. Meanwhile, geographic expansion and rising healthcare demand in emerging markets support its long-term growth outlook. Even in the short term, despite all the concerns around it, Merck continues to post growth from its Keytruda franchise.

Buy Merck

While headwinds from China sales, impending LOE, and risks from competition, pipeline execution, and regulatory and pricing pressures make strong cases against MRK, the correction from the top makes for a GARP-style entry at these levels. The healthy free cash flow and time in hand will not only enable flexibility to acquire strategic partners but support a very healthy dividend yield of 4.16%.

Merck is a clear Buy at current valuations for long term, GARP, and dividend-focused investors, balancing between short-term support from its defensive positioning and long-term optionality.

data.title: AMD: Valuation Is Attractive Now, Shifting To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) is a leading U.S. fabless semiconductor chip stock. I wrote about the stock last year at a time when the stock was trading at a mid-40s forward P/E range, a multiple I thought too pricey. Since I issued a sell recommendation the stock has fallen steadily with the share price down over 50% in early April from the time of my previous report.

I am switching to a buy recommendation today on the back of a few factors. I will go into detail on these below but in essence what we have seen is a rapidly falling share price, earnings forecasts holding up favorably, new opportunities in the AI space emerging, continued CPU market weakness from Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) and a starting valuation entry point which is no longer excessive.

Taking these factors together, I think now is the right moment to close my sell call on AMD and switch to a buy from this point. I feel the stock looks a compelling buy at current levels, the current valuation has only been available to investors a handful of times in the past decade and arguably the forward opportunity set today offers the most upside in a decade.

Valuation

First despite some downgrades to EPS forecasts, the stock price has fallen far faster than expected earnings. As a result AMD is now trading at a discount to both its five and ten-year average forward P/E. More strikingly is the change in the PEG ratio, when I wrote about AMD last July the stock was trading on a punchy PEG of around 6x. Today investors can pick up shares for a PEG just over 1x which is the cheapest the stock has been in years.

What makes me even more bullish on this valuation is the current consensus EPS forecasts have not adjusted yet for the EPS upside associated with AMD's newly announced Middle East deals, most notably the $10B joint venture with the Saudi HUMAIN national AI company. I see current EPS forecasts as likely underestimating AMD's earnings power, which in turn means the already highly attractive entry valuation would look even more so if EPS forecasts were fully up-to-date.

Valuation is always a bread and butter element of investing for me. The greatest company in the world can be a bad investment if the valuation is too expensive. AMD has swung significantly over the past year from being too expensive for my blood, to looking very attractive today. The attractive entry point on offer for investors is compelling, I think AMD looks the best value in quite a few years.

New Opportunities

The initial phase of AI development was very much concentrated in the data centers of a select few hyperscale cloud players. Media reports suggested the market for AI GPUs was completely dominated by Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). However, as the market has expanded both within the hyperscale players and beyond, there has been an appetite to avoid a complete reliance on a single vendor. As a result, a mixture of customer silicon, ASICs offered by the likes of Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) and alternative GPU solutions such as AMD have gained traction. The acquisition of ZT Systems, which closed in March, enhances AMD's ability to compete with peers offering full rack-scale solutions for data center compute.

In their most recent quarter AMD reported growth in their data center segment of 57%, delivering revenue of $3.7B. While they omit a breakout of the exact split between data center CPU and GPU revenue, the numbers show a clear indication of demand. Product cadence remains furious with the next iteration of the MI-series of GPUs due for bulk production in mid-2025. Even though AMD is likely to remain a distant second to Nvidia in the data center GPU, the firm still has plenty of room to achieve high growth, especially with the ecosystem of customers expanding.

Last week we got the significant news of an AI partnership between a host of leading U.S. companies and Middle Eastern countries, in particular Saudi Arabia. As part of the slew of announcements, we saw AMD CEO Lisa Su as part of the U.S. delegation in Riyadh. AMD for their part signed a $10B joint venture agreement with the newly formed Saudi national AI company called Humain. This type of deal invariably will involve the supply of chips and software initially, but as we know the pace of hardware development is furious, so client countries will seek to upgrade their chips in a regular cadence, much as a data center cloud company does.

The deal, while momentous in itself, potentially presages a new wave of AI demand from rich sovereign nations looking to own and operate AI infrastructure on their own soil. Given the options countries have are essentially the U.S. or Chinese chip companies, I think its clear that U.S. companies with their tech expertise are well positioned to capture this demand. I could easily foresee a situation where industrialized countries such as Japan would seek to strike similar deals to the Saudis.

PC CPUs are an area where AMD has seen substantial market share gains in recent years. The segment is a bit boring compared to data center GPU, but it does tell a story of solid execution by AMD. The ten years ago the firm had a market share of about 9%, in a market completely dominated by Intel. Today that share stands just shy of 30%, an incredible 3x improvement in just ten years. Perhaps the grinding of market share gains over time in CPU could serve as a template for the company to go after Nvidia's GPU dominance. I expect AMD to continue their good run and gain incremental share in CPU as more and more customers find better value in AMD vs their legacy preference for Intel.

Risks

AMD's steady progress in both server and client/PC CPU has at least in part been a function of weakness at Intel. The traditional CPU leader has struggled with its operations and this culminated with the departure of CEO Pat Gelsinger at the end of 2024. A renewed management team at Intel could potentially lead to a turnaround of operations there and a better execution in the CPU market. This does not necessarily mean AMD would cede hard-won share, but it could make gaining further market share incrementally more difficult, especially given Intel's traditional brand advantage, especially with enterprise customers.

AMD, like other tech hardware players, is a cyclical business, the demand for the firm's products naturally waxes and wanes with the economic cycle. The important thing, of course, is the firm's ability to growth earnings consistently through an economic cycle, such that the next peak is higher than the prior and the next trough higher than the previous. If we enter a recession, it is unlikely AMD will have anywhere to hide. Cyclical stocks tend to underperform the market in a downturn as their earnings exhibit more volatility than a healthcare stock, for instance.

We are potentially reaching a point of peak capex by the large hyperscale cloud players, a trend which cannot continue forever. The introduction of sovereign wealth money such as the Saudis helps to de-risk the potential demand destruction in a recession and AMD's valuation is no longer scary expensive, so we have some support in that respect. I like AMD as an investment from this point, but I advise readers the stock will likely be volatile, as evidenced by its five-year beta of close to 2.

Conclusion

Having held a sell recommendation on AMD for almost a year, I am switching my recommendation to a Buy given an evolved picture. AMD is no longer trading at a lofty valuation, especially when the level of growth forecast is considered. The firm's acquisition of ZT systems sets it up to better compete with peers like Nvidia in offering full rack offerings to data center customers.

The AI universe of opportunities has expanded significantly in the past year, with new market entrants such as Middle Eastern sovereign AI players expanding the pie for all chip players. The presence of Lisa Su in Riyadh as part of the U.S. delegation was a clear indication that AMD is well positioned to avail of the next leg of AI spending, given where the stock is trading today, I like the setup from here.

MARKET REVIEW

While the U.S. equity markets were weak in the first quarter, emerging market equities demonstrated resilience, with the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (the Index) gaining 2.9%. A weakening dollar and an investor shift away from the U.S. amid trade policies and concern about looming tariffs were tailwinds.

In the Index, Information Technology was the only negative sector, while Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services advanced double digits.

With respect to the Index’s larger country weights, China, Brazil, and Korea outperformed, while Taiwan, Indonesia, and India declined.

The PGIM Jennison Emerging Markets Equity Opportunities Fund (MUTF:PDEZX) declined slightly in the quarter, returning -1.09%, underperforming the Index, as growth stocks collectively underperformed value stocks in the quarter.

SECTOR PERFORMANCE

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index underperformed the MSCI All-Country World Index (ACWI) but outperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. during the quarter.

Within the Index, Information Technology was the only negative sector.

Consumer Discretionary, Communication Services and Materials gained the most.

The PGIM Jennison Emerging Markets Equity Opportunities Fund returned -1.09% for the quarter and underperformed the Index.

Communication Services holdings added value in the quarter, driven by strong gains in Kanzhun (BZ). The Chinese on-line recruitment platform that utilizes artificial intelligence to match job seekers to recruiters benefited from better-than-expected recruitment demand.

Consumer Discretionary was another area of strength.

  • BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) reported robust earnings growth for 2024 driven by auto and mobile parts and assembly revenues and offshore expansion.
  • Laopu Gold, a luxury jewelry company in China, is enjoying strong sales momentum and higher store productivity.
  • Declines in India’s MakeMyTrip (MMYT) offset some of the gains. Shares were down without a meaningful catalyst. It continues to benefit from a dominant market share and industry tailwinds.

Industrials detracted in aggregate, but with mixed results for individual holdings.

  • Shares of Brazil’s Embraer (ERJ), the world’s third largest plane maker, rose following reports that it delivered 75 aircraft in the fourth quarter, up 27% from the third quarter. For all of 2024, there were 206 aircraft delivered, representing a 14% increase from 2023.
  • Transformers and Rectifiers manufactures transformers to help India increase its power supply by offering more advanced power solutions. Shares were down perhaps due to profit taking and concerns about the overall industry. We eliminated the position.

Varun Beverages drove underperformance in Consumer Staples due to intensifying competitive pressures, and the position was sold from the Fund.

Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) hurt results in Financials. Argentina’s largest bank is executing well with discipline on the efficiency side, despite restructuring charges affecting its operating expenses in 4Q24.

The Information Technology sector modestly underperformed.

  • An underweight in Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) was beneficial as it was hurt by several headwinds—the January earthquake in Taiwan, U.S. tariffs, and potential for disruption from DEEPSEEK. The team’s long-term thesis remains intact.
  • Another Taiwanese company, Wiwynn declined in tandem with a sell-off of AI infrastructure companies. We eliminated the position due to its exposure to AI servers.

Portfolio Positioning

During the past quarter, the team made several key allocation shifts at both the country and sector level in response to evolving macro conditions and relative valuations. It is worth noting that outside of some semiconductor exposure, there has been very little exposure to trade or tariffs.

The Fund’s China exposure nearly doubled this quarter, driven by growing confidence in the government’s pro-growth policy pivot that began in late 2024. Recent concrete measures were enacted—including increased fiscal stimulus, targeted support for AI and technology innovation, and a more accommodative monetary stance. These signal a shift away from the restrictive posture of the past two years. We also believe the release of DeepSeek may revitalize technology investments and lead to an earnings upcycle in Chinese internet and technology. The team selectively added to names in industrial automation and next-gen platforms that are trading at attractive multiples with strong medium-term earnings potential.

The team also increased our allocation to Brazil, reflecting an improving macro picture—falling inflation, stabilizing interest rates, and positive real rates—alongside idiosyncratic opportunities in Financials and Industrials.

On the other hand, we significantly reduced the Fund’s India exposure. While the structural growth story remains intact, elevated valuations relative to current earnings potential prompted U.S. to take profits and rotate into better risk/reward opportunities. We’ll look to re-enter at more attractive entry points.

We decreased the Fund’s position in Taiwan, with a focus on reducing exposure to semiconductor and AI supply chain names.

At the sector level, we increased our weight in Communication Services, where we see improving fundamentals, lower regulatory risk, and valuation upside. This was funded in part by trimming Information Technology exposure—exiting names correlated to AI infrastructure that had come under pressure related to the release of the Deep Seek model in January.

Outlook

As of the end of the first quarter, we continue to see a favorable earnings outlook across emerging markets and believe the corrections seen out of developed markets, particularly the U.S., could be beneficial for the asset class.

An accommodative or easing monetary policy globally as well as a softening U.S. dollar should also be tailwinds, while emerging markets’ overall valuations remain very attractive on both an absolute and relative basis.

After several years of underperformance and economic strain, China appears well-positioned to outperform given a government shift toward pro-growth; however, as always, we are focused on idiosyncratic ideas relative to the Fund’s opportunity set.

Despite several positives, as U.S. tariffs unfold, there will likely be additional volatility and uncertainty, which could have a meaningful impact on global growth and ultimately on emerging markets.

The Fund continues to focus on companies with strong business models that can deliver earnings growth through this period at reasonable valuations.
